statement: Inside `engine-temporal`, the most maintainable provider-portable architecture is a three-layer split: core execution logic, a capability registry, and provider adapters, with a generic attempt-ledger envelope plus `provider_extensions` for provider-specific fields.
rationale: The repo already centralizes provider metadata and services, but only for OpenAI. A Temporal rewrite needs stronger separation so provider-specific request shapes, cache semantics, and usage reconciliation do not leak into workflow logic.
keyAssumptions: The engine will continue to need a shared attempt ledger and shared observability surface across providers
Provider-specific metadata can be safely isolated into extension payloads without undermining debugging or replay
validationCriteria: Core workflow code does not directly construct provider wire payloads
New provider support mostly adds a registry entry, one or more adapter implementations, and provider-specific tests
Provider-specific fields in attempts remain queryable without distorting the generic top-level schema
critiquePoints: If the adapter boundary is too narrow, provider-specific recovery logic may leak back into core orchestration
If the extension payload is unstructured, debugging and analytics may degrade
questions: What minimum generic operation types should core execution know about in v0: sync inference, tool loop, structured extraction, and a capability hook for async batch?
Which provider-specific fields deserve first-class columns in `llm_attempts`, and which should stay in `provider_extensions`?
